data.title: Waymo Vs. Zoox Vs. Tesla Vs. Pony - We Think Elon Musk Will Lose
data.description:
Waymo (NASDAQ: GOOGL) reports that it's crossed 250K paid rides per week in the United States. That's a business that's now earning more than $100 million per year. As the "self-driving wars" head up, the purpose of this article is to highlight who we think the winners will be (Google) and the losers (Tesla) (NASDAQ: TSLA).

The List (and Business Importance)

With the end of market leader Cruise, a sign of how technologically complicated this problem is, the list of serious competitors in self-driving is effectively 4 long in our view.

  • Waymo (backed by Google)
  • Tesla
  • Zoox (backed by Amazon AMZN)
  • Pony.AI (PONY)

The companies in the list vary pretty dramatically in where they are. Waymo has a fully operating self-driving business with hundreds of cars in major U.S. cities. Zoox has custom-built self-driving vehicles running driverless testing, as the company has moved past retrofitting vehicles, but it's not actually earning money yet.

Tesla has earned billions from FSD subscriptions sold on its cars and has leading "cruise control" technology but isn't running large-scale FSD trials yet. Pony.AI has made major partners, including with Uber recently, and similarly doesn't have large-scale driving yet, but is also focused on autonomous trucking in China.

Backing up here, one aspect worth highlighting is the importance of success to the company.

Let's take Amazon. Amazon paid $1.2 billion for Zoox, and has invested in it since. However, as a $2 trillion company, if Zoox were to disappear tomorrow, it would make up well below 1% of Amazon's value. Similarly, Waymo's latest funding round put it at a $45 billion valuation, or just over 2% of Google's valuation which is "something" but not needle moving.

On the other extreme you have Pony.AI, a $5 billion publicly traded company after its IPO last year with one goal. In between you have Tesla, who's CEO Elon Musk has said it's key to the company's future success and current valuation, but who's current business is manufacturing and selling cars.

Technological Approaches

There's also a lot of variance in technological approaches. Everyone is using artificial intelligence and some aspect of sensor fusion.

However, Tesla's Elon Musk has said so far that the company will not use Lidar and only use camera technology. There are questions about whether a camera-only approach works (Tesla is facing investor lawsuits for its claims) and exactly how that pans out remains to be seen. However, it's worth noting it's not that Tesla doesn't have access to Lidar, it's just not including it for now.

At the end of the day, we don't see one technological approach as ground-breaking versus the other.

Self-Driving Miles Driven

An indicator of how these companies are performing is how many miles are they actually driving with self-driving and how are they performing with it. The below statistics are from the California DMV, but California is the leading state for self-driving miles.

In terms of miles per disengagement, a statistic to indicate how far they go before a human takes over, there's pretty clear jumps between the leaders and the ones behind. Note that Cruise no longer exists as a competitor, and the same is true for Apple. It's also worth highlighting there is a reason that Tesla isn't here, the company didn't actually test full self-driving in 2024.

Another major piece of these is how many miles are the companies driving. Waymo is the clear leader here, earning revenue, with almost 2.4 million miles driven. For perspective, that's ~0.0001% of all miles that're driven on U.S. roads. Zoox, with its miles per disengagement and almost 1 million miles with its Amazon backing is another clear choice.

Nuro is an underdog in both miles driven and miles per disengagement, with a $6 billion valuation that was a down round from 4-years ago. Pony.AI leads in disengagement but is driving fewer miles. However, we do want to highlight that Waymo and Zoox are both leading the pack, and Tesla isn't testing at all.

Total Addressable Market

In terms of total addressable, the market is huge, with Americans driving more than 3 trillion miles per year on all roads.

Waymo is young, but its pricing ranges from $0.2 / mile on top of its flat flee to $1.2 / mile in San Francisco and Los Angeles. That implies the potential of hundreds of billions to trillions of dollars worth of revenue. Putting an exact profit margin on that is incredibly difficult, however, we do want to highlight that we think it'll be a very competitive business.

Tesla did its own analysis, reportedly suppressed by Elon Musk, that figured with competition it would be hard to ever drive a true profit from the service, and it would be a very volatile rollout. We're not particularly surprised at all with that conclusion.

Specifically, should more than one company hit full self-driving, when you don't have hiring employees etc. in the cost basis, the self-driving becomes a scaling problem. Waymo appears to have recognized this, it's partnered to build a new factory in Arizona with the ability to build thousands of vehicles. The company is scaling, but it's a slow and steady process.

Tesla has the scale for manufacturing that its peers don't have, however, with minimal self-driving projects from the traditional mega manufacturers after Cruise (i.e. Toyota, Honda, etc.) we don't think it'll be hard for Waymo etc. to expand partnerships as demand grows. Zoox builds its own robotaxis but it's backed by Amazon, a company that knows a thing or two about scaling.

Attempting to put a valuation on it though, vehicle costs per mile are ~$0.81. We presume self-driving at scale will need to be less than that. Zoox's operating costs are roughly $0.42 / mile, and we expect companies to move towards self-driving. At 3.4 trillion miles per year, that would imply maximum profits of just over $1 trillion.

Assuming 25% of that to justify switching by drivers and that's still $10s of billions in potential annual profit.

Our View

For those who're looking to invest in the self-driving revolution, we recommend investing in either Waymo or Zoox through Alphabet or Amazon. These are both companies that're at the forefront of self-driving, Waymo is actively driving revenue, and they're scaling up permits / handling the very real process of carrying users.

Waymo has already driven customers for millions of miles with no major accidents, which is better than human drivers. Pony.AI is also doing well, but it's a smaller company without a big financial backer. However, it is a pure-play investment, which enables investors to potentially earn big rewards at the cost of big potential losses.

However, the company we don't recommend investing in this list is Tesla. Specifically for 2 reasons:

1. Poor Market Positioning

As discussed above, Tesla isn't leading in self-driving testing. The company isn't operating full customer-centric self-driving yet and at the same time, competitors like Waymo are ramping up vehicle counts. First-mover advantage is a big deal and that's a real concern.

2. Reward vs. Valuation

We discuss this more in our article in Tesla here, however, the majority of Tesla's valuation hinges with success in self-driving. That means that not only is the upside more limited, but the downside is substantial.

3. Lawsuit Risk

The company has already promised self-driving to numerous users and it's facing lawsuits over claims that it has made. Even Grok, through xAI, knows self-driving was first promised almost a decade ago. That increases the potential risks and downside.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the underdog. Our view is that increasingly it looks like self-driving is a bunch of patched together incremental improvements and solutions that make it better than a human driver and enable it to scale, there's no single magical breakthrough that enables flawless self-driving.

However, should such a break-through emerge, at an underdog, it could upend the market and give a single company a commanding share.

Conclusion

The self-driving wars are scaling up. That's despite numerous dedicated companies having a lower valuation today than they had several years ago. However, Waymo is now earning more than $100 million per year in revenue and scaling up rapidly, the company has recently announced deals for a new factory to scale up.

Despite that, in every new field of discovery, there are differences between opportunity and overvalued. For those looking to invest, we see Google and Amazon as effectively managing the downside risks while retaining upside potential given their opportunities. We see Tesla as an overvalued company in the segment and a poor investment.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Japan: Getting Exposure To India On The Cheap
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA, & Samuel Rines

When Suzuki entered India in 1982, it didn't just launch a car company - it helped write the first chapter of a new industrial story. In partnership with the Indian government, it formed Maruti Udyog, a modest joint venture that would eventually become Maruti Suzuki (OTCPK:SZKMY) - now a market titan controlling roughly half of India's passenger vehicle market. Four decades later, Suzuki's India bet hasn't just paid off; it has redefined the center of gravity for the company. India isn't a growth market for Suzuki - it's the market, its largest globally.

And Suzuki isn't alone. Hitachi (OTCPK:HTHIY), long known for cooling and refrigeration in India's sweltering summers, has quietly pivoted into the software space through its acquisition of GlobalLogic - marking a deeper integration into India's booming IT sector. Sharp and Casio have carved out their own lanes in consumer electronics and appliances.

But the real connective tissue often goes unnoticed: the Japanese trading houses - Mitsubishi (MUFG), Mitsui (SMFG), Sumitomo (SMFG), Itochu (OTCPK:ITOCF) - the very firms Warren Buffett has quietly been accumulating. These conglomerates don't shout, but they shape. They've helped fund and coordinate ventures in specialty chemicals, steel and infrastructure - like Nippon Steel's partnership with Tata Steel (OTC:TATLY). Meanwhile, in transport, Hitachi and Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (OTCPK:MHVYF) are bringing Japanese precision to India's rapidly expanding rail and metro systems.

Today, Japanese firms aren't just manufacturing or building infrastructure in India - they're branching into the digital, the logistical and even the agricultural. Rakuten has set up R&D hubs, signaling Japan's ambitions in e-commerce and digital services. Yamato Transport, Japan's logistics heavyweight, is forming alliances that hint at a deeper supply chain integration. Japanese breweries are entering the Indian market, while trading giants like Mitsui are deploying capital into the backbone of agriculture - cold storage, distribution networks and farm-to-market systems.

What began as a focused presence in autos and electronics has evolved into a broad-spectrum strategy. The Japanese playbook in India is no longer sector-specific; it's ecosystem-wide. This is what long-term commitment looks like - starting with a beachhead and then quietly compounding into something far more expansive.

This isn't just economics - it's alignment by design. As the Indo-Pacific takes center stage in global geopolitics, India and Japan find themselves not just trading partners but strategic complements. Both see the value in a stable, open, rules-based regional order - and both recognize the need for balance as China's assertiveness grows more visible.

What's emerging is a kind of strategic flywheel: shared security interests deepen trust, that trust fuels more investment, and that investment tightens the partnership further. It's diplomacy powered by economics - and economics shaped by geopolitics. The result is a relationship that's no longer reactive but compounding.

One of the clearest signals of this deepening alignment is the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue - the Quad - linking Japan, India, the U.S. and Australia. It's often framed as a geopolitical counterweight, but beneath the surface, it's also an economic architecture in the making. The Quad is about securing supply chains, shaping technology standards and building infrastructure outside the gravitational pull of Chinese capital.

For India and Japan, it has unlocked a new level of coordination - across 5G, semiconductors and other strategic technologies. The trust built at the top - among heads of state and national security advisors - filters down to boardrooms and R&D labs. In sectors like telecom and AI, Japanese and Indian firms are now forming partnerships not just because it makes business sense but because the geopolitical foundation is aligned. When governments are in sync on trusted networks and supply chain resilience, it becomes easier for companies to move fast - and move together.

Another key piece of the puzzle is the Supply Chain Resilience Initiative (SCRI), launched by Japan, India and Australia as a direct response to the hard lessons of the pandemic and the systemic risks of overdependence on China. On the surface, it's a policy agreement - but in practice, it's a strategy to rewire the plumbing of global trade.

The idea is simple: combine complementary strengths to build a more shock-resistant Indo-Pacific. Japan brings capital and manufacturing precision. India offers scale and labor depth. Australia adds raw materials and geographic balance. Together, they're creating new pathways - investment forums, policy alignment, even cross-country supplier matchmaking - to shift critical links in the supply chain away from single-country exposure.

For India, this means an influx of manufacturing investment. For Japan and Australia, it means a more diversified and dependable supply base. The SCRI isn't just a hedge - it's a new operating model for regional trade built on shared strategic logic.

Behind the headlines, a quiet architecture has been taking shape. Since 2006, the "India-Japan Strategic and Global Partnership" - upgraded in 2014 to "Special Strategic and Global Partnership" - has built a durable institutional framework: 2+2 ministerial dialogues, annual leadership summits and deep-working groups focused on everything from defense to trade. This scaffolding keeps the relationship on track even as governments and global conditions change.

But it's not just pageantry. Political alignment at the top has real-world spillovers - accelerated approvals for Japanese industrial zones, yen-denominated loans to cushion currency risks and smoother regulatory pathways for joint ventures. It's diplomacy that translates directly into lowered friction and faster execution.

The bigger picture? India and Japan are not just cooperating - they're synchronizing. Security and economics are no longer separate lanes; they're running in parallel. In a world where geopolitical alignment increasingly dictates economic opportunity, this partnership stands as one of the clearest examples of that convergence in action.

Strategic Implications and Future Outlook

What's unfolding between India and Japan isn't just bilateral trade - it's a blueprint for strategic convergence in the 21st century. By investing in each other, Japan and India are co-authoring a vision for regional stability and shared prosperity - one that deliberately moves beyond the gravitational pull of any single dominant power.

Japan's approach - long-term, patient and embedded - has earned it more than just economic returns. It has bought influence, credibility and a seat at the table of India's rise. This isn't extractive capital; it's participatory. Japan doesn't just sell to India - it builds with India, hires locally and co-develops infrastructure that endures.

For Japan, India represents a democratic, if messy, hedge against geopolitical concentration - a youthful, fast-growing market with immense upside. For India, Japan is proof that global partnerships can be high-trust, high-impact and long-term.

The India-Japan story is what it looks like when strategic intent aligns with economic substance. It shows how resilient capital, layered with geopolitical foresight, can reshape not just economies but the very architecture of the Indo-Pacific. With the groundwork laid, the next chapter could move faster than the last. The compounding has already begun.

India andJapan as Investment Options

To contextualize the aforementioned ideas, it's worthwhile to take an investment exposure to Japanese export-oriented companies like the WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ) and compare it to an investment exposure to broad-based, profitable companies across India with the WisdomTree India Earnings Fund (EPI).

Figure 1: Standardized Returns

From a high level, the intuition regarding Japan is that it is a highly educated, technologically advanced society, but one that has a slow-growth timbre and a demographic quite advanced in age. India is largely the opposite - a younger population seeking to become more and more technologically advanced as the country with the largest overall population in the world.

  • On a year-to-date basis, DXJ and EPI saw returns that were similarly negative, and then on a 5-year and 10-year basis, these strategies saw returns that were similarly positive.
  • The 3-year returns were the most highly differentiated. We saw a depreciating yen and an environment that put Japan's exporters very much in favor.

Figure 2: 3-Year Returns Stood Out

A perennial issue with India is that people get excited about its prospects and expect things. The narrative of a "young, highly educated population" is repeated time and again, and the economic growth statistics in the range of 5%–6% get people who are used to 1%–2% growth in developed markets quite excited. This pushes equity valuations for India's market higher.

Japan, on the other hand, is known as the slow-growth, economic laggard in many respects. Even if we can cite facts and statistics where this simply isn't true, it creates an advantage for Japan's equities in that they can quietly maintain a lower valuation.

We can see in figure 3:

  • DXJ usually has a lower estimated P/E ratio than EPI, at least over the past 10 years.
  • The current picture shows DXJ roughly 25%–30% below EPI on an estimated P/E ratio basis.

Figure 3: 10Years of Valuation History (DXJ vs. EPI)

True, the majority of Japan's revenues are not coming from India, but if Japan's companies are leaning into the India growth story and there are avenues to access the equities at a 25%–30% discount on certain measures, Japanese equities, exemplified through DXJ, may be quite worthy of consideration for those looking at how the Asia story could evolve from 2025 to 2030.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. As these Funds have a high concentration in some sectors, the Funds can be adversely affected by changes in those sectors. Due to the investment strategy of these Funds, they may make higher capital gain distributions than other ETFs. Please read the Funds’ prospectuses for specific details regarding the Funds’ risk profiles.

DXJ: The Fund focuses its investments in Japan, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments in Japan that can adversely affect performance. Investments in currency involve additional special risks, such as credit risk and interest rate fluctuations. Derivative investments can be volatile and may be less liquid than other securities and more sensitive to the effect of varied economic conditions.

EPI: This Fund focuses its investments in India, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments associated with the region that can adversely affect performance. Investments in emerging, offshore or frontier markets such as India are generally less liquid and less efficient than investments in developed markets and are subject to additional risks, such as risks of adverse governmental regulation and intervention or political developments.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy”.

Original Post

data.title: Beijing Capital International Airport: Rating Downgrade On Trade War Risk
data.description:
Airport infrastructure offers an opportunity to capitalize on demand for air travel without being exposed to the volatility that airline stocks offer through cost and top line fluctuations. In September, I initiated coverage for Beijing Capital International Airport (OTCPK:BJCHY). The stock price development has been favorable with a 30% increase matching my price target. The listing in Hong Kong under the ticker 694:HK provides higher volumes and more stable pricing and can be considered a better reference to measure performance. The listing in Hong Kong gained 27% since my prior report, which is also in line with the expected direction of the stock price.

The company profile discussed below is largely derived from my prior report on the company, updated with the most recent figures available:

Beijing Capital International Airport: Capacity To Grow To More Than 200 Million Passengers

Beijing Capital International Airport or BCIA is one of the two main airports for the city of Beijing. The airport processed 67.4 million passengers in 2023, making it the 16th busiest airport in the world. For reference purposes, the airport processed over 100 million passengers in 2019 putting it in the second spot of busiest airports in the world.

In 2019, Beijing Daxing International Airport commenced operations and by 2024, it processed 49.4 million passengers bringing the Beijing traffic to 116.8 million. Compared to 2019, passenger numbers increased 13% for both airports in Beijing.

The opening of Daxing Airport should not be viewed as competition to Beijing Capital International Airport as Beijing Capital International Airport Company Limited operates Daxing as well as Beijing Capital International Airport which provides a combined capacity for more than 200 million passengers when fully developed.

Pre-pandemic, the expectation was that by 2040 demand for air travel in Beijing would reach 235 million, so these two airports will be serving passengers in parallel rather than the new airport replacing the older airport and development to expand the Daxing Airport will likely take place eventually.

Trade Provides A Near Term Pressure For Airport Infrastructure

I previously discussed risks such as pandemics and economic downturns as risks. Right now, with a trade war raging, we should focus on what the implications could be for Beijing Airport. There is no way to quantify the impact at this stage, but the reality is that trade wars could affect consumer confidence and reduce disposable income. So, there could be an impact on domestic travel, which grew 17.4% in 2024 measured by passenger throughput.

Tariffs that are applied to Chinese goods could also make doing business in China less attractive, partially offset by China intensifying trade with countries other than the US while international travel may be less affordable for Chinese citizens. There is a risk that cargo throughput (+29.3% in 2024) and international travel (+103% in 2024) will be pressured.

So, competition is not a main risk and the main risk would be a global economic downturn or anything that would be similar to the pandemic while in the long-term traffic growth should positively impact revenues.

Beijing Capital International Airport Losses Narrow

Revenues increased from RMB 4.6 billion to RMB 5.5 billion. Aeronautical revenues grew 27% while non-aeronautical revenues, which includes retail income, leases, real estate and parking, experienced 15% growth. Growth was supported by 27% higher passenger throughput.

EBITDA increased by roughly RMB 1 billion, bringing the EBITDA margins to 21% from 0.3% a year ago. In 2019, margins were north of 40% according to the income statement.

Operating expenses declined 1%. So, the revenues fully passed through to the bottom line. There still was a RMB 347 million loss, but this was significantly better than the RMB 1.4 billion loss. Pre-tax losses improved from RMB 1.7 billion to RMB 629 million.

Beijing Capital International Airport is currently in the phase of expanding the passenger throughput and aircraft movement at little to no added costs. The airport has about a third of its recovery in passenger traffic that has yet to be completed, and that is growth that should flow to the bottom line in a favorable way.

For the first quarter, for which we have no detailed insights on passenger traffic growth, sales grew only 1%. The pre-tax loss tapered from RMB 149.2 million to RMB 125 million.

Beijing Capital International Airport Company Receives Rating Downgrade

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Before I comment on the valuation of the stock, I want to point out that the stock price targets are based on the listing in Hong Kong, which provides better volumes than the alternative OTC tickers. The listing in Hong Kong under the ticker 694:HK provides volumes running in the millions rather than BJCHY which has volumes of hundreds of units every few days. The BJCHY ticker represents 5 ordinary shares, contrary to BJCHF which represents one ordinary share but has higher volumes allowing for better selling and buying in desired quantities and desired prices. So, if you want to translate the price targets projected to price targets for BJCHY, multiply the target by five.

Given the increase in the stock price, more in-depth modelling of results and lower expected results in 2024 and forecast for 2025, I am downgrading the stock from buy to hold. In 2024, EBITDA was 25% lower than expected and for 2025 the estimates have come down by 22%. This still provides annual growth of 34%, but we also note that free cash flow in 2024 was not as strong as expected. So, there is a lot of pressure, driven by lower than expected earnings in 2024 and downward revisions. On top of that, in the operational environment, the trade war also infuses risk. Therefore, I am downgrading the stock from buy to hold.

Conclusion: BCIA Has Upside, But Risk Increased

I believe that the full year revenues were very strong and showed the upward potential on margins, as revenues should be able to increase on a cost neutral basis. The first quarter revenues grew in a somewhat underwhelming way, but the operating losses still tapered. However, if we combine that with the trade risks, I do believe that the risk profile has become less attractive. As a result, I do believe it is fair to apply a hold rating to the stock now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Glencore: Rising Metallurgical Coal Production Should Support Share Price In 2025
data.description:
Glencore (OTCPK:GLCNF) is a commodity giant whose share price has more than halved since its early 2023 peak. The time has come to give it a fresh look and decide if the time has come to turn bullish.

A quick overview of Glencore and its businesses

Glencore plc is a Switzerland-based commodity producer and trader with operations across the globe. The company is primary listed in London (LSE: GLEN) and Johannesburg (JSE: GLN) so that if you plan to use the US-listed ticker GLCNF, option trading will not be accessible to you. This may be a detail for most readers but an important one to keep in mind if you are a covered call lover for instance.

Glencore produces and trades a wide range of commodities. The company refers to its trading activities as "Marketing activities" and to its production activities as "Industrial activities", as show in Figure 1. The company also differentiates between operations centered on "Metals and minerals" and those focused on "Energy and steelmaking coal" (Figure 2).

Production the most important contributor to earnings but volatility extremely high

Over the past four years, most of the earnings have come from the production business units and not from the trading units, even if trading revenues were more than three times larger. This is primarily explained by higher margins for the production units. Indeed, when Glencore produced commodities in2024, its profit margin fell between 26% and 49% against a 1.2%-2.9% range for its trading activities. In short, the performance of the production units has a major impact on the company's total earnings.

Though, while trading activities' earnings are relatively stable, those stemming from commodity production are highly volatile. Hence, even if we exclude the exceptional year of 2022 when commodity prices skyrocketed following the invasion of Ukraine by Russia, Glencore has made on average US$3.8 billion annually from trading activities since 2019 and US$8.11 billion from production activities. While the standard deviation from this average is about 12% for trading earnings, it jumps to almost 46% when it comes to production earnings. To tell things differently, it is statistically expected for production earnings to jump or fall by as much as the total trading earnings every single year! If we include calendar year 2022 in the calculations, the expected annual fluctuation even jumps to US$6.6 billion, equivalent to 173% of the trading earnings' average! This is why EPS projections for the company are generally really wide. In the case of Glencore, production results are what differentiates a bullish quarterly report from a disappointing one and this is where our attention should focus on.

Production units: everything comes down to coal and copper

Obviously, all the commodities produced by Glencore do not weigh equally on its earnings. Figure 4 below clearly highlights Glencore's reliance on copper and coal, the two commodities that were responsible for more than 85% of Glencore's production earnings in 2024.

About Copper Production

Glencore is involved not only in mining copper ore but also in its concentrating, smelting, casting and refining activities. Hence, in 2024, the company produced not only 951,600 metric tons of copper but also 440,800 tons of anodes and 463,600 tons of cathodes. Of all its copper mining operations, the Collahuasi facility in Chile and the Antamina mine in Peru are by far the most profitable. In 2024, profit margin, based on adjusted EBIT, was 47.9% for Collahuasi and 43.8% for Antamina. Unfortunately, the volume of copper produced at these two mines is not exactly thriving (Figure 5) and Glencore even expects its overall copper production to fall in 2025 by between 4.37 and 10.67% compared to 2024. Not really encouraging, especially when the Q1 production report shows a risk to seeing this target to be cut even further.

About Coal Production

Since its merger with coal company Xstrata in 2013, Glencore has remained continuously involved in coal production and trading. The company not only rejected a plan to spin-off its coal activities but has also increased its exposure to metallurgical coal by purchasing 77% of the coal assets owned by Canadian firm Teck Resources.

Traditionally, Glencore has been a major player in thermal coal but not in metallurgical coal. Generally speaking, thermal coal, or steam coal, is used in power plants or other industrial boilers to produce heat which is then turned into electricity. Metallurgical coal, as its name indicates, is used in the production of steel. Metallurgical coal is first turned into metallurgical coke (this is why this type of coal is usually referred as coking coal) which is then mixed with iron ore and limestone in a blast furnace to produce iron. Because of the numerous grades of coal, the boundary between thermal and metallurgical is sometimes quite artificial and this is why there are many exceptions to this general picture. For instance, sponge iron producers in India use thermal coal from South Africa and not metallurgical coal. Still, keeping this difference in mind is useful when it comes to Glencore.

As shown by Figure 6, conversely to copper, Glencore's coal production has been rising over the past 4 years and is expected to fall between 117 and 130 million metric tons this year, against 119.5 million mt in 2024. Beyond production increase, Glencore seems committed to reducing the share of thermal coal in its overall coal production. This share, which has traditionally been standing above 90%, fell to 83% in 2024 and is expected to fall below 75% in 2025. Why is distinction important? Well, simply because margins for these products are vastly different.

According to 2024 results, Glencore mined a total of 100.4 million metric tons of thermal coal and these operations produced a total revenue of US$10.142 billion. This translates into an average sales price of US$101 per metric ton produced. Meanwhile, adjusted EBIT came out at US$1.464 billion so that average profit margin for Glencore when it comes to thermal coal production was 14.4% in 2024. Let's compare this with metallurgical coal, which traditionally commands a much better sales price. In 2024, the company produced a total of 19.9 million metric tons whose sales generated US$3.79 in revenue, or US$190.45 per ton. The adjusted EBIT for metallurgical coal production came out at US$1.044 billion, which translated into a profit margin of 27.5% or about twice the one associated with thermal coal. It is then not surprising to see Glencore try to increase its exposure to metallurgical coal.

Outlook for copper and coal prices

You may wonder why earnings are so volatile when it comes to Glencore's production units. The answer lies in a simple answer: the global sales prices of the commodities produced is itself highly volatile. It is then important to have a broad understanding of the current pricing trend for coal and copper.

Copper price

Figure 7 below shows the copper spot price, per pound, as recorded on COMEX since 2010. The trend is clearly bullish, even if the market is somehow struggling to push price sustainably above US$5/pound. The truth is, this breakout may not even appear in 2025, as some analysts expect the global copper market to be characterized by oversupply. If confirmed, this may lead to weaker copper prices in the coming months, even if the long-term bullish trend remains firmly in place.

Coal price

When it comes to coal prices, the distinction must once again be made between thermal and metallurgical coal prices. Newcastle coal index price, the reference price for top quality thermal coal in Asia, where most of the demand for thermal coal is now concentrated, is back below US$100 per metric ton, where it was before the price of energy commodities exploded in 2022 following the invasion of Ukraine by Russia (Figure 8). At around US$100 per ton, high-cost producers or producers whose coal quality is below standards, have started reducing output. This includes Glencore's Cerrejon operations in Colombia, which will be scaled down by at least 5 million tons this year. While thermal coal prices are not expected to jump back above US$200 per metric ton anytime soon, they are at the same time not expected to plunge significantly in the near future due to steady demand across Asia, despite decarbonization pressures, and very few greenfield or even expansion projects under development. In short, steady demand coupled with reduced supply should prevent thermal coal prices from falling significantly, especially for high-quality thermal coal as the one produced by Glencore at Hunter Valley Operations.

Figure 9 below shows how price for best-quality metallurgical coal, the so-called Premium Hard Coking Coal made in Australia, has been fluctuating since December 2015. Like thermal coal, metallurgical coal price rose significantly in 2022 before slowly retreating and falling back below US$200 per metric ton. Metallurgical coal price is highly reliant on Chinese demand, the country responsible for more than 50% of the world's total crude steel production. The Chinese economy has not be roaring for quite some time and this clearly keeps on weighing on metallurgical prices. Besides, while India is expected to become the next giant steel producer, demand for foreign metallurgical coal is not picking up as quickly as many have expected, even if the country only produces 15 to 20% of all the coking coal it consumes each year. As a result, some analysts have been expected the global metallurgical coal market to enter a state of oversupply in 2025. This would suggest that metallurgical prices may actually fall back within the US$100-150 per metric ton range later this year.

Glencore: shareholder-friendly policies to partially offset earnings volatility

Let's briefly summarize our findings so far: Glencore is a company whose financial results are highly correlated to the performance of its copper and coal production units. These units' performance depends on both the amount of commodity produced and its sales price. For 2025, the outlook for copper is negative when it comes to production and possibly neutral-to-negative when it comes to price. Regarding coal, Glencore is expected to produce more metallurgical coal and less thermal coal this year. While bearish pressure on thermal coal seems limited, the risk to see lower metallurgical coal prices will increase if combined demand from China and India continues to disappoint.

As shown by Figure 10, Glencore's P/E ratio tends to turn negative fairly often, reflecting the volatile nature of its commodity businesses. Tough, it is important to underline here that Glencore's results are actually more volatile than peers BHP and Anglo American, the other two major diversified miners producing both copper and coal. The fact that Glencore has reported negative earnings 40% of the time over the past 10 years, against 20% for Anglo American and 10% for BHP, probably explains in large part the gap in P/E ratio with its competitors. Indeed, if we calculate the 10-year P/E ratio average for the three companies, using only positive data as input, we obtain a result of 16.63 for Glencore, of 19.98 for BHP and of 22.73 for Anglo American.

Despite the large fluctuations in annual earnings, Glencore remains committed to rewarding shareholders with attractive dividends and a continuous buyback of shares. For 2025, the company plans to spend US$1 billion on its share repurchase program. This should roughly reduce the free float by about 10 to 15%. Regarding debt, the 2024 increase is mostly explained by the purchase of Teck's metallurgical coal assets in Canada. Neither the level nor the trajectory is currently worrisome, even if the company's cost of finance has increased by 22% in 2024 (Figure 11).

Is Glencore undervalued?

Using the elements highlighted so far, let's try to simulate Glencore's share value for 2025 and consequently determine if its current price is justified.

Trading earnings

Glencore earnings from its trading units have averaged US$3.33 billion between 2019 and 2024, excluding the exceptional year of 2022. Seeing that the standard deviation is US$480 million, we can assume that trading earnings should fall into the US$2.85 and US$3.81 billion this year.

Production earnings

Using the company's latest production guidance (Figure 12) and assuming that both coal and copper prices will either rise or fall by a maximum of 20% in reference to current level, it is possible to simulate earnings for Glencore's copper and coal units for 2025. In this simulation, It is also assumed that production cost will increase by 5% for both products this year and that the total earnings stemming from the production of commodities other than coal and copper will remain stable.

Other assumptions

The simulation also makes the following assumptions:

  1. the cost of finance for the company remains stable in 2025;
  2. the loss associated to "Significant Items" will fall towards its 11-year average of minus US$2.9 billion;
  3. the buyback program for 2025 will be completed;
  4. average P/E ratio for the year will be 14; and
  5. GLCNF dividend for the entire year will be US$0.2.

Results

The results of the simulation are provided by Figure 13 below and suggest that Glencore's share price, currently at about US$3.3 when it comes to GLCNF, may indeed be undervalued, assuming that our worst-case scenario (i.e., a drop of 20% in the average sales price for both copper and coal in 2025) does not materialize. Even in that case, the bearish pressure on share price should be limited due to dividend payment and share buyback. With Glencore's actual share price being so close to the lower band of our 2025 simulated range (US$3.16), risk appears to be clearly on the upside for this ticker and share price may rebound significantly if coal and copper prices hold around current level or even slightly increase over the next 12 months.

About downside risk

While the picture looks bullish for Glencore's share price, it is worth considering how this positive outlook could be severely derailed. There are two clear and major risks worth underlining here. First: a large drop in metallurgical coal output, especially from Canada, where most of Glencore's production comes from. Glencore has published an optimistic coal output guidance for 2025 that could be scaled down in the coming months in case of a major weather event. Experience from the former operator, Teck Resources, shows that coal railing capacity in British Columbia can easily be reduced in case of wildfires or flooding, forcing coal production to be cut by sometimes as much as 20%. Second, the 2025 loss for "Significant Items" could come way higher than currently expected. The "Significant Items" section includes all the infrequent events that have a significant impact on the company's financials. This includes, for instance, impairments, or fair value adjustments for inventories. In 2024, the loss associated with this section reached 5.32 billion or 144% of the company's adjusted earnings! As shown by Figure 14, it is really difficult to predict the potential loss associated with these "Significant Items" due to extreme volatility. Our simulation assumed a loss of US$2.9 billion for 2025, which is the 10-year average, but the market would likely penalize Glencore if this number once again exceeds the company's adjusted EBIT.

Conclusion

Glencore's results are highly reliant on its production of copper and coal (both thermal coal and metallurgical coal). Even if global prices for these three commodities further consolidate this year, bearish pressure on the company's share price should soon start weakening due to rising metallurgical coal production, continuous dividend payment and an aggressive share buyback policy. Glencore is a BUY at current level.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fidelity Select Consumer Staples Portfolio Q1 2025 Review
data.description:
Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Select Consumer Staples Portfolio is a sector-based, equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.
  • Our core philosophy is that stock prices follow earnings and returns over the long term. We look for durable franchises capable of compounding value and delivering above-average earnings growth over time.
  • Investment opportunities arise when there is a differentiated view with regard to the long-term earnings power of a company. We focus on stocks where the market underestimates the magnitude or duration of growth and the valuation is attractive.
  • Fundamental research is key to our approach, as we build the portfolio stock by stock and take a multiyear investment perspective to get a differentiated view on long-term performance drivers. We also look to capitalize on short-term market opportunities, with the goal of optimizing risk-adjusted returns for shareholders.
  • Sector strategies could be used by investors as alternatives to individual stocks for either tactical- or strategic-allocation purposes.
PERFORMANCE SUMMARY Cumulative Annualized
3 1 3 5 10 Year/ LOF1
Month YTD Year Year Year
Select Consumer Staples Portfolio Gross Expense Ratio: 0.69%2 0.65% 0.65% 1.43% 3.76% 10.78% 5.98%
S&P 500 Index -4.27% -4.27% 8.25% 9.06% 18.59% 12.50%
MSCI US IMI Consumer Staples 25/50 4.11% 4.11% 9.96% 6.43% 12.56% 8.44%
Morningstar Fund Consumer Defensive 1.93% 1.93% 2.83% 2.99% 11.43% 7.05%
% Rank in Morningstar Category (1% = Best) -- -- 56% 43% 67% 57%
# of Funds in Morningstar Category -- -- 26 26 25 20

1LifeofFund(LOF)ifperformanceislessthan10years.Fund inception date: 07/29/1985.

2This expense ratio is from the most recent prospectus and generally is based on amounts incurred during the most recent fiscal year, or estimated amounts for the current fiscal year in the case of a newly launched fund.Itdoesnotincludeanyfeewaiversorreimbursements,whichwouldbereflectedinthefund'snetexpense ratio.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate; therefore, you may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance stated. Performance shown is that of the fund's Retail Class shares (if multiclass). You may own another share class of the fund with a different expense structure and, thus, have different returns. To learn more or to obtain the most recent month-end or other share-class performance, visit fidelity.com/performance, institutional.fidelity.com, or 401k.com. Total returns are historical and include change in share value and reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any.

Cumulative total returns are reported as of the period indicated.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Market Review

The consumer staples sector gained 4.11% in the first quarter, according to the MSCI U.S. IMI Consumer Staples 25/50 Index. This result notably outperformed the -4.27% return of the broad-based S&P 500® index (SPX, SP500), as the defensive-oriented sector rose when post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs.

Amid this backdrop, the MSCI sector index gained 1.82% in January, as investors reacted favorably to the definitive election outcome and potential policy changes, including the prospect of tax cuts, expansionary fiscal policy and lower regulation. The appeal of defensive investments grew stronger in February, when volatility spiked and investors shunned growth-oriented securities as post-election momentum lost steam. Consumer staples stocks gained 4.45% for the month. However, staples then slid in March (-2.10%), as investors shifted away from defensive sectors following data showing that inflation climbed 2.8% in February from a year earlier – higher than the Fed's 2% target, more than economists expected and exceeding January's annual pace – as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers continued to slip, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead.

Among the biggest segments in the consumer staples index, consumer staples merchandise retail – which accounted for 30% of the index in Q1 – roughly broke even, held back by Walmart (WMT) (-3%) and Target (TGT) (-22%). Meanwhile, membership club Costco Wholesale (COST) gained roughly 3%. Household products, making up 19% of the benchmark, rose about 2%, helped by Kimberly Clark (KMB) (+9%).

Tobacco (+26%) and soft drinks & non-alcoholic beverages (+10%) each handily topped the sector, making up 24% of the benchmark. Tobacco was primarily driven by Philip Morris International (PM) (+33%) and Altria Group (MO) (+17%).

Conversely, distillers and vintners (-16%) saw the sector's weakest performance this quarter, hindered by Constellation Brands (STZ) (-16%) and Brown Forman (BF.B)(BF.A) (-10%).

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 0.65%, lagging the 4.11% advance of the MSCI U.S. IMI Consumer Staples 25/50 Index.

Security selection and industry positioning both detracted from the fund's performance versus the sector index for the quarter, especially an underweight in the strong-performing tobacco segment, as well as a large overweight in distillers and vintners. Our picks in consumer staples merchandise retail also hurt.

From the tobacco group, a large underweight in Philip Morris International, best known for its Marlboro cigarettes, was the biggest individual relative detractor. Shares of Philip Morris benefited from quarterly earnings and an initial 2025 financial forecast that was better than most analysts expected, driven by strength in the company's oral-pouch product Zyn. We meaningfully reduced exposure to the stock, which was the No. 2 underweight as of March 31.

Conversely, positioning in soft drinks & non-alcoholic beverages, especially an overweight, helped. Here, outsized exposure to Coca-Cola (KO) (+16%) was the biggest individual contributor. We've been bullish on the company for quite some time, based on its revenue acceleration and improved fundamentals, despite the stock price not reflecting this for most of 2024. Our view was finally justified in February, when the stock jumped after the beverage giant posted Q4 financial results that exceeded management's forecast and outshined peers. As of March 31, Coca-Cola was the top holding and No. 2 overweight.

In the same segment, a large overweight in Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) (+8%) notably helped. Shares of the beverage company gained after management reported stronger-than-expected organic sales growth for the fourth quarter. Analysts viewed the company's annual outlook favorably, especially given concerns about coffee price inflation. Keurig was the third-largest holding and top overweight as of March 31 as we believe the company has effectively maintained price increases and consistently delivered top-line growth.

Outlook and Positioning

As Q2 begins, we believe business dynamics within the consumer staples sector have mostly returned to normal following post-pandemic doldrums, though we are keeping a close eye on how tariff policies may shift the landscape. Aside from that, we see the sector remaining a defensive anchor amid the current uncertainty.

Trends in the sector are not particularly strong, but they are not disastrous. The recent significant headwinds related to the high cost of raw materials and commodities are largely in the past, except for a few commodities, including chocolate, cocoa and coffee, which have substantially increased in price. With that said, we are excited about the potential to drive the fund's relative result with stock picking, given the sector's rich landscape, including wide valuation spreads, which may present opportunities to capitalize on turnaround stories or undervalued stocks.

We believe this environment may allow consumer staples stocks to perform favorably versus the broader market in the year ahead. In managing the fund, we plan to continue to focus on core fundamentals and traditional industry drivers, with a preference for companies operating in a favorable market structure and maintaining a strong underlying growth profile. For example, we believe Procter & Gamble and Coca-Cola could benefit from consistent, albeit boring, financial delivery against lower expectations. The two are the top holdings as of March 31.

Since taking office on January 20, President Trump has rolled out an aggressive tariff plan, with more details set to be announced in early April. Its effect on the consumer staples sector has been varied through the end of March, and it's still too early to fully assess the longer-term impact. Tariffs on imports from Canada and Mexico, for instance, have been largely softened by carve-outs and workarounds under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, reducing their immediate impact on U.S. companies.

Meanwhile, tariffs on Chinese imports have already started to bite. Although incremental, these tariffs have hurt consumer electronics, with retailers Walmart and Target feeling the brunt of the impact. Tariffs on aluminum have led to noticeable price increases in canned goods and aluminum foil. Looking a bit further down the line and the potential impact on the consumer staples sector, we believe Trump's tariff policies will increase input costs and consumer prices, and they could lead to higher inflation.

While this scenario is undoubtedly negative for consumer staples in absolute terms, it likely would position the sector as a relative safe haven compared to growth sectors that are less defensive during an economic downturn. The key will be how companies navigate these hurdles by managing costs and pricing strategies in response to the evolving tariff landscape. This is something we are monitoring and discussing with the business leaders we visit.

Regardless of the market backdrop, the fund's positioning and our core investment strategy are largely unchanged. We continue to focus on choosing relative "winners" and "losers" within each industry. This stock selection process is based on building robust financial and operating models that we believe give us an edge in forecasting earnings revisions for companies. We supplement this fundamental work with industry-level analysis to get ahead of broader trends that could influence the sector.

The past three months, we maintained the fund's large overweight positions in soft drinks & non-alcoholic beverages and distillers & vintners. We believe the beverages segment, particularly non-alcoholic beverages, is the best market structure in consumer staples, based on its high barriers to entry, relatively tepid competitive pressure and fragmented customer base.

Before investing in any mutual fund, please carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For this and other information, call or write Fidelity for a free prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus. Read it carefully before you invest.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Views expressed are through the end of the period stated and do not necessarily represent the views of Fidelity. Views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and Fidelity disclaims any responsibility to update such views. These views may not be relied on as investment advice and, because investment decisions for a Fidelity fund are based on numerous factors, may not be relied on as an indication of trading intent on behalf of any Fidelity fund. The securities mentioned are not necessarily holdings invested in by the portfolio manager(s) or FMR LLC. References to specific company securities should not be construed as recommendations or investment advice.

Diversification does not ensure a profit or guarantee against a loss.
